slides.title: Now, this slide explains the concept of lateral resolution — sometimes called cr
bullets: If two scatterers in the body — say, two small reflecting points — are separated by a distance smaller than the beam width, their echoes will overlap when received by the transducer. When that happens, the signals blend, and we can’t tell them apart. That’s what you see on the right side of the figure: the two curves merge into one, and the system records them as a single echo.

On the other hand, if the two scatterers are separated by a distance greater than the beam width — specifically, greater than the full width at half maximum — then the two echoes appear as two distinct peaks. That’s what we want. So the smaller the F-W-H-M, the better your lateral resolution, because you can separate two adjacent points in space.

So in summary, lateral resolution is determined primarily by the beam width. Narrow beams resolve closely spaced objects; wide beams cause overlap. This concept applies directly to your image sharpness — particularly in the direction perpendicular to the beam path.
slides.fullText:
Now, this slide explains the concept of lateral resolution — sometimes called cross-beam resolution — and it’s illustrated again by a figure from your textbook. Remember earlier, we talked about the beam profile and how it has a certain width, which we describe using the full width at half maximum, or F-W-H-M. Ideally, we want this width to be as small as possible, because that determines how well we can distinguish two objects that are side by side.

If two scatterers in the body — say, two small reflecting points — are separated by a distance smaller than the beam width, their echoes will overlap when received by the transducer. When that happens, the signals blend, and we can’t tell them apart. That’s what you see on the right side of the figure: the two curves merge into one, and the system records them as a single echo.

On the other hand, if the two scatterers are separated by a distance greater than the beam width — specifically, greater than the full width at half maximum — then the two echoes appear as two distinct peaks. That’s what we want. So the smaller the F-W-H-M, the better your lateral resolution, because you can separate two adjacent points in space.

So in summary, lateral resolution is determined primarily by the beam width. Narrow beams resolve closely spaced objects; wide beams cause overlap. This concept applies directly to your image sharpness — particularly in the direction perpendicular to the beam path.
slides.script: Lateral resolution is the ability to distinguish two points side by side, and it's limited by the beam width, which is described by the full width at half maximum, or F-W-H-M. The smaller the beam width, the better the lateral resolution, and the more accurately we can locate structures in the body. If the points are too close together, their echoes will overlap, and we can't distinguish them. This is a fundamental limit in ultrasound imaging.

slides.title: Now let’s talk about ultrasound focusing, because focusing is one of the main wa
bullets: The easiest analogy is photography.
Just like a lens can focus light to a small spot, an ultrasound transducer can focus sound to a small region.
This figure is showing the idea of a focal distance and a focal plane.
The beam narrows as it approaches the focus, reaches its smallest width near the focal plane,
and then it spreads out again beyond that point.
Now compare the two cases on the right.
With strong focusing, the beam converges more aggressively.
So the focal spot is tighter, meaning the beam width at the focus is smaller.
That gives you excellent lateral resolution at that depth.
But the trade-off is that the good resolution is confined to a relatively small depth range,
because the beam diverges more quickly after the focus.
With weak focusing, the beam narrows more gently.
So the focal spot is not as tight, meaning the best lateral resolution is not as sharp.
But the beam stays more uniform over a larger depth range,
so the resolution is more consistent across depth.
So you should think of focusing as a trade-off:
strong focusing gives you a sharp focus over a short depth of field,
while weak focusing gives you a broader depth of field with less peak sharpness.
To quantify focusing strength, both optics and ultrasound often use an f-number.
On the slide it is defined as:
f-number equals R divided by 2a,
where R is the radius of curvature of the focusing surface,
and a is the radius of the aperture.
So a smaller f-number means stronger focusing,
because you either have a smaller radius of curvature, which is more strongly curved,
or you have a larger aperture, which can concentrate energy more effectively.
And in practice, this is directly tied to lateral resolution:
a tighter focus generally improves lateral resolution at the focal depth,
but you always have to balance that against the usable depth range for the imaging task.

slides.title: Alright, we’ve talked about lateral resolution, which is the ability to separate
bullets: Now let’s talk about axial resolution, sometimes called longitudinal resolution,
which is the ability to separate two targets along the beam direction, along depth.
This figure explains the key idea.
In ultrasound imaging we do not transmit a continuous wave.
We transmit a short pulse.
That pulse has a finite length in space, and a finite duration in time.
Now imagine there are two reflecting boundaries along the beam path, labeled A and B.
When the pulse reaches boundary A, part of the pulse is backscattered and returns to the transducer.
The rest of the pulse continues forward, reaches boundary B, and also generates an echo.
Here is the critical point.
If A and B are very close, the two echoes come back so close in time that they overlap.
When the echoes overlap, the system cannot distinguish them,
and they appear as one blended reflection.
So axial resolution is basically the minimum separation between A and B
that produces two echoes that are distinct in time.
That minimum separation is set by the pulse duration.
If the pulse is long, the echoes overlap easily, and axial resolution is poor.
If the pulse is short, the echoes are more separated in time, and axial resolution improves.
That is why the formula on the slide is so simple:
axial resolution equals one half times pulse duration times c,
where pulse duration is the time length of the transmitted pulse,
and c is the speed of sound in the tissue.
And the one-half factor is there because ultrasound is a round-trip measurement.
The echo time includes the travel to the reflector and the travel back.
So only half of that travel distance corresponds to the actual spacing between two reflectors.
So the takeaway is very direct:
to improve axial resolution, we want a shorter pulse.
That is why damping and bandwidth matter in transducer design,
because they help create shorter pulses.
So in one sentence:
axial resolution is controlled mainly by pulse length,
and shorter pulses give finer detail along depth.

slides.script: As we send a short pulse along the beam direction, it travels until it hits a reflecting boundary, like A or B, and bounces back, creating an echo. The time it takes for the pulse to reach a boundary and return determines the depth of that boundary. The distance between the pulse's transmission point and the reflecting boundary is calculated using the speed of sound and the total travel time. This is how we determine the depth of internal structures.

slides.title: Now, let’s talk about image contrast in ultrasound imaging. The term “contrast”
bullets: We use the prefixes “hyper” and “hypo” to describe this difference in the scattering amplitude of echoes. When a tissue produces echoes that have a higher scattering amplitude than the average, we call it “hyperechoic.” That means the reflected signal is stronger than normal, and it shows up as a bright region on the ultrasound image. Conversely, when the tissue has a lower scattering amplitude than average, we call it “hypoechoic.” In that case, the reflected signal is weaker, and it appears darker on the image.

These variations in brightness — from hyperechoic to hypoechoic — are what give us contrast. They allow us to distinguish one structure from another. For example, on the ultrasound image shown here, you can see how the liver, renal sinus fat, renal pyramids, and fluid-filled bowel loops all appear with different brightness levels. The renal sinus fat appears bright because it is hyperechoic — it reflects ultrasound strongly. The renal pyramids or fluid-filled bowel loops appear darker because they are hypoechoic — they scatter less energy.

Some tissues, like the liver or renal cortex, tend to have uniform, mid-level echogenicity, meaning they reflect ultrasound in a moderate, consistent way. Others, like the anterior surface of the liver or the anterior surface of the kidney, are more reflective, showing distinct bright boundaries on the image.

So, in summary — the way ultrasound waves interact with various tissues determines the level of brightness on the image. Hyperechoic areas are brighter and correspond to stronger scatterers. Hypoechoic areas are darker and correspond to weaker scatterers. This difference in signal amplitude is the foundation of contrast in ultrasound imaging.

However, sometimes the natural contrast between tissues is not sufficient to clearly separate structures or identify pathology. In such cases, we use contrast agents to improve visibility. One of the most effective types of contrast agents in ultrasound imaging is the microbubble. These microbubbles, typically ranging from two to five micrometers in diameter, are introduced into the bloodstream to enhance contrast. They act as additional reflective surfaces that increase backscattering, especially in regions where the natural contrast is weak.

By introducing microbubbles, we can significantly improve the visualization of internal structures, making it easier to detect lesions, tumors, or subtle tissue changes. This approach is particularly useful in clinical cases where precise imaging is needed to make accurate diagnoses.

These microbubbles, typically ranging from two to five micrometers in diameter, are introduced into the bloodstream to enhance contrast. They act as additional reflective surfaces that increase backscattering, especially in regions where the natural contrast is weak.

By introducing microbubbles, we can significantly improve the visualization of internal structures, making it easier to detect lesions, tumors, or subtle tissue changes. This approach is particularly useful in clinical cases where precise imaging is needed to make accurate diagnoses.
slides.script: When a tissue produces echoes that have a higher scattering amplitude than the average, we call it hyperechoic, meaning it appears brighter than usual. On the other hand, if a tissue produces echoes with a lower scattering amplitude, it's called hypoechoic, appearing darker than usual. This contrast is essential for clinical interpretation, as it helps us distinguish between different types of tissue.

slides.title: Now let’s talk about microbubbles as ultrasound contrast agents.
bullets: Microbubbles are tiny, typically on the order of two to five micrometers in diameter.
They are encapsulated, usually with an albumin shell or a lipid shell.
And they are often filled with a high–molecular weight gas,
which helps them remain stable in the bloodstream.
Once these microbubbles are injected intravenously, they circulate through the vascular system.
And when an ultrasound wave reaches them, something special happens.
Because a microbubble contains gas surrounded by liquid,
there is a very strong acoustic mismatch at that interface.
So compared to surrounding tissue, microbubbles scatter ultrasound very efficiently.
They also “ring” in the ultrasound field.
In other words, they undergo volumetric oscillation:
they expand and contract in response to the alternating pressure of the wave.
That oscillation produces a strong echo signal, and often a nonlinear response as well.
Practically, what you see is that blood becomes much easier to visualize.
Perfusion, small vessels, and microvasculature can appear brighter and more distinct,
because the microbubbles boost the backscattered signal coming from the bloodstream.
And clinically, ultrasound systems can use special contrast or “bubble-specific” imaging presets.
These modes are designed to emphasize the microbubble signal while suppressing background tissue,
so you can focus on vascular flow and perfusion more clearly.
So the key takeaway from this slide is:
microbubbles are engineered scatterers.
Their size and gas core make them respond strongly to ultrasound,
and that lets ultrasound highlight blood and perfusion in ways that standard imaging cannot.

slides.title: Now let’s move one step further — using microbubbles not just for imaging, but a
bullets: This concept is known as acoustically enabled drug delivery. The idea is both simple and powerful. Suppose we inject a drug together with microbubbles into the bloodstream. These microbubbles circulate throughout the body, including through the small vessels, or microvasculature, around a tumor site.

When we know the approximate location of a tumor, we can focus ultrasound waves precisely on that region. The ultrasound energy — tuned to the right amplitude and frequency — causes the microbubbles in that area to oscillate vigorously. If we increase the energy slightly, the bubbles rupture or collapse, releasing their drug payload right at the target site.

This process delivers the medication directly to the tumor, minimizing side effects and maximizing treatment effectiveness. It’s like having millions of tiny delivery capsules that release the drug only when and where an ultrasound activates them.

This technique opens the door to an entirely new field known as theranostics — combining therapy and diagnostics. With this approach, ultrasound imaging can both visualize and treat disease simultaneously. The precision and non-invasiveness of this method make it an extremely promising direction for the future, particularly for treating cancers and other localized diseases.

So, using ultrasound and microbubbles together, we can not only image internal structures in great detail but also control drug delivery dynamically — by sound waves themselves. This is an exciting frontier in biomedical engineering and medical imaging.

slides.title: Alright, let’s wrap up today’s lecture. As we conclude, I’d like to give you the
bullets: You’ll be working on two related problems, both designed to reinforce your understanding of the transducer and matching layer concepts we discussed earlier. Please refer to your green textbook for additional details and supporting equations — it’s not difficult, and I’m confident you can handle it.

The first problem, number three point five, asks you to plot the transmitted frequency spectrum of an ultrasound beam from a transducer operating at a central frequency of one point five megahertz. Assume that the transducer is damped, and then repeat the plot for the beam returning to the transducer after passing through tissue and being backscattered.
The second problem, number three point six, focuses on the concept of the matching layer, which is one of the most important topics we covered today. The question is:

“To improve the efficiency of a given transducer, the amount of energy reflected by the skin directly under the transducer must be minimized. A layer of material with an acoustic impedance Z M L is placed between the transducer and the skin. If the acoustic impedance of the skin is denoted by Z S, and that of the transducer crystal is denoted by Z C, show mathematically that the value of Z M L that minimizes the energy of the reflected wave is given by Z M L equals the square root of Z C multiplied by Z S.”
This derivation is straightforward, and you can find the full explanation in your textbook. The due date for this homework is one working week from today, so please make sure to submit it on time.

We’ll continue our discussion next time, where we’ll wrap up our study of ultrasound imaging. Remember — the matching layer is crucial, both conceptually and practically, because it determines how efficiently energy moves from the transducer into the body. Understanding it deeply will also help you as we move toward more advanced imaging systems in the upcoming lectures.
